What's the best exercise for abs?

Real talk: abs are made in the kitchen, not the gym. You can do 1000 crunches and never see them if your body fat is high. That said — for building the actual muscle:

🔥 Plank variations — best for core stability
🔥 Dead bug — protects your lower back
🔥 Ab wheel rollout — advanced but brutal
🔥 Hanging leg raises — great for lower abs

Crunches are overrated. Compound lifts like squats and deadlifts work your core harder than most ab exercises anyway. Sort your diet and the abs will show.

Should I buy a home gym or gym membership?

It depends on several factors. Gym membership makes sense if: you benefit from social environment, you want variety of equipment, you're a beginner learning proper form, or you don't have space at home.

Home gym makes sense if: you have the space and budget for basic equipment, time efficiency matters (no commute), you prefer training alone, or you train consistently enough to justify the investment.

For most people starting out, a gym membership offers better value. What's your situation — space, budget, and how long have you been training?

Should I do cardio before or after weights?

After. Always after, if your goal is strength or muscle. Here's why: lifting requires maximum energy and neuromuscular focus. Cardio before depletes both.

Exception: if your main goal is endurance performance (marathons etc.), reverse it. For fat loss or muscle building: weights first, cardio after. Keep cardio to 20-30 minutes post-weights and you're good.

Hamstring, it happened yesterday during sprints

Okay — rest it for 48-72 hours minimum. No stretching the hamstring yet, that makes it worse. Ice for 20 minutes every few hours today. After 72 hours, gentle walking is fine.

You CAN still train upper body and core while it heals. Don't use injury as an excuse to do nothing — just adapt. Come back to legs in 5-7 days with light work only. If the pain is severe or you heard a pop, see a doctor immediately.

What is muscle soreness and is it normal?

What you're experiencing is called DOMS — Delayed Onset Muscle Soreness. It typically peaks 24-72 hours after training and is caused by microscopic damage to muscle fibres during exercise — this is normal and part of the adaptation process.

DOMS indicates you've provided a new stimulus to your muscles. It doesn't necessarily mean a better workout — experienced athletes get less sore as they adapt.

Management: light movement (walking, easy cycling), adequate protein, hydration, and sleep. Do NOT stretch aggressively when sore — gentle mobility work is fine. And you can absolutely train through mild soreness.

I'm 50 years old, is it too late to get fit?

Absolutely not — and this is backed by extensive research. Adults who begin resistance training in their 50s can achieve muscle gain and strength improvements comparable to younger individuals. The body's adaptability remains throughout life.

What may differ: recovery takes slightly longer, so we'd build in adequate rest days. Joint health becomes more important, so exercise selection matters. And consistency over intensity is key.

What are your main goals at this stage?

I have lower back pain, can I still train?
Lower back pain is very common, and in most cases you can still train — but carefully. First important question: is this a diagnosed condition (disc issue, sciatica) or general muscular tightness?
Just tightness I think, no diagnosis

Muscular lower back tightness often responds very well to the right exercise. Avoid: heavy loaded spinal flexion (sit-ups, bent-over rows with poor form), heavy deadlifts until pain resolves.

Focus on: hip hinge patterns, glute strengthening, and core stability. Strong glutes take load off the lower back significantly.

I'd recommend starting with the 'Lower Back Relief' routine in the Luvit library — mobility work that also builds strength. And please see a physio if the pain persists beyond 2 weeks.

How do I do a proper squat?

THE most important exercise — let's get it right. Stand feet shoulder-width, toes slightly out. Push hips BACK first, then down — like you're sitting in a chair behind you. Keep chest up, knees tracking over toes. Go as deep as your mobility allows — ideally thighs parallel or below.

Most common mistake: knees caving inward. If yours do, work on glute activation first.

What does progressive overload mean?

Progressive overload is the foundational principle of all strength and fitness training. It means gradually and consistently increasing the demands placed on your body over time.

Practically, this means:
📈 Adding weight (e.g. 2.5kg more each week)
📈 Adding reps (e.g. going from 8 to 10 reps)
📈 Adding sets (e.g. going from 3 to 4 sets)
📈 Reducing rest time
📈 Improving technique

Without progressive overload, the body adapts and stops changing. The Luvit app's workout log helps you track this automatically — you can always see if you're lifting more than last week.

Should I buy a home gym or gym membership?

It depends on several factors. Gym membership makes sense if: you benefit from social environment, you want variety of equipment, you're a beginner learning proper form, or you don't have space at home.

Home gym makes sense if: you have the space and budget for basic equipment, time efficiency matters (no commute), you prefer training alone, or you train consistently enough to justify the investment.

For most people starting out, a gym membership offers better value. What's your situation — space, budget, and how long have you been training?

What is muscle soreness and is it normal?

What you're experiencing is called DOMS — Delayed Onset Muscle Soreness. It typically peaks 24-72 hours after training and is caused by microscopic damage to muscle fibres during exercise — this is normal and part of the adaptation process.

DOMS indicates you've provided a new stimulus to your muscles. It doesn't necessarily mean a better workout — experienced athletes get less sore as they adapt.

Management: light movement (walking, easy cycling), adequate protein, hydration, and sleep. Do NOT stretch aggressively when sore — gentle mobility work is fine. And you can absolutely train through mild soreness.

After. Always after, if your goal is strength or muscle. Here's why: lifting requires maximum energy and neuromuscular focus. Cardio before depletes both.

Exception: if your main goal is endurance performance (marathons etc.), reverse it. For fat loss or muscle building: weights first, cardio after. Keep cardio to 20-30 minutes post-weights and you're good.

How do I do a proper squat?

THE most important exercise — let's get it right. Stand feet shoulder-width, toes slightly out. Push hips BACK first, then down — like you're sitting in a chair behind you. Keep chest up, knees tracking over toes. Go as deep as your mobility allows — ideally thighs parallel or below.

Most common mistake: knees caving inward. If yours do, work on glute activation first.

Check out 'Perfect Squat Form' in the Luvit library — watch it before your next leg day. How deep can you currently squat?

📈 Add reps or sets
📈 Reduce rest times

What's the difference between cardio and HIIT?

Good question. Traditional cardio (also called steady-state or zone 2) means sustained effort at moderate intensity — 60-70% of max heart rate — for 20-60 minutes. Think jogging, cycling, swimming at a conversational pace.

HIIT (High Intensity Interval Training) alternates between very high intensity bursts (85-95% max heart rate) and short rest periods. A typical session is 15-25 minutes but burns similar calories to 40+ minutes of steady cardio.

Both have value. Zone 2 builds aerobic base and is great for recovery. HIIT builds fitness faster and is time-efficient. For most people, a mix of both works best. What are you training for?

What's the difference between cardio and HIIT?

Good question. Traditional cardio (also called steady-state or zone 2) means sustained effort at moderate intensity — 60-70% of max heart rate — for 20-60 minutes. Think jogging, cycling, swimming at a conversational pace.

HIIT (High Intensity Interval Training) alternates between very high intensity bursts (85-95% max heart rate) and short rest periods. A typical session is 15-25 minutes but burns similar calories to 40+ minutes of steady cardio.

Both have value. Zone 2 builds aerobic base and is great for recovery. HIIT builds fitness faster and is time-efficient. For most people, a mix of both works best. What are you training for?

What does progressive overload mean?

Progressive overload is the foundational principle of all strength and fitness training. It means gradually and consistently increasing the demands placed on your body over time.

Practically, this means:
📈 Adding weight (e.g. 2.5kg more each week)
📈 Adding reps (e.g. going from 8 to 10 reps)
📈 Adding sets (e.g. going from 3 to 4 sets)
📈 Reducing rest time
📈 Improving technique

Without progressive overload, the body adapts and stops changing. The Luvit app's workout log helps you track this automatically — you can always see if you're lifting more than last week.

How important is rest and sleep for fitness?

Extremely important — arguably as important as the training itself. Muscles don't grow during the workout; they grow during recovery, particularly during deep sleep when growth hormone is released.

For most people training 3-5 days per week: 7-9 hours of sleep per night is ideal. Less than 6 hours consistently has been shown to significantly reduce muscle protein synthesis and increase cortisol (which breaks down muscle tissue).

Are you currently getting enough sleep?
I sleep about 5-6 hours on weekdays

That's below optimal for performance and recovery. Even one extra hour can make a measurable difference. Some practical suggestions: set a consistent sleep time, avoid screens 30 minutes before bed, and keep your room cool and dark.

If sleep is genuinely limited, it's worth reducing training volume slightly to avoid overtraining. Quality over quantity always applies.
